summary: I grew up in Texas, which was very conservative and segregated by law when I was young. I didn't fully understand the segregation until my senior year in college, but it definitely shaped my views on race and social justice. My family background was traditional, with my grandfather being a sheriff, but I also had a southern liberal influence through my church. Texas was the place where my early experiences with race and social divisions first took root.

I attended the University of Texas at Austin after two years of junior college, starting in the late 1950s. The university was integrated, but housing was not, which pushed me toward involvement with the YWCA where I met many black students. I pursued my master's in English there and became a national student Y officer, which deepened my engagement with race and peace issues. It was also at UT Austin that I met Connie Curry and got involved with the sit-in movement and southern student organizing.

The YWCA was a crucial part of my college experience at the University of Texas. It was integrated and provided housing and support for women, which was important since university housing was segregated. Through the YWCA, I met many black students and became involved in liberal and civil rights activities. I also became a national student Y officer, attending regional and national meetings focused on peace, race, and meaningful workplaces, which helped shape my activism.

The National Student Association played a big role in my early activism. Connie Curry received a grant through the NSA to recruit southern student leaders for a human relations seminar, which I attended in Minneapolis-Saint Paul. The NSA meetings were where I met many key figures and where the debate about supporting sit-ins took place. It was also at an NSA congress that I gave a speech as a southern white student supporting the sit-ins, which helped launch my involvement in the liberal caucus organized by Al Haber.

Connie Curry was instrumental in recruiting me into the sit-in movement and southern student organizing. She ran a southern student relations group funded by the National Student Association and took a group of southern student leaders to a human relations seminar. I remember sitting with her in a restaurant in Austin, crying as she told me about the sit-ins. Connie also helped organize the second SNCC meeting in Atlanta, which I attended, and I knew her well from these early civil rights activities.

I was connected to the Southern Student Organizing Committee through my involvement with Connie Curry and the southern student leaders she recruited. The SSOC was part of the broader southern student activism scene that supported civil rights sit-ins and organizing efforts. While I was more directly involved with the YWCA and SNCC, the SSOC was part of the network of southern student groups pushing for racial justice during the early 1960s.

I became deeply involved with SNCC starting around 1961-62, traveling to southern campuses and recruiting for race relations workshops. I worked closely with Ella Baker and other SNCC leaders, taking minutes at meetings and helping organize Friends of SNCC on northern campuses. I lived and worked in Mississippi at the Freedom House, participating in the literacy project and the Mississippi Summer Project. SNCC was central to my activism and organizing efforts throughout the early to mid-1960s.

Al Haber was a key figure I met at the National Student Association congress where he organized the liberal caucus supporting progressive issues like the sit-ins. This caucus was where many of us who later formed Students for a Democratic Society came together. Al's organizing helped create a space for liberal students to push for civil rights and social change in the early 1960s.

Tom Hayden was someone I met at the NSA congress and later got engaged to and married in Austin. We worked closely together on civil rights and student activism, including organizing food drives for SNCC and participating in SDS activities. Tom was the SDS secretary and a key figure in the movement, and we shared many experiences traveling and organizing from Texas to Ann Arbor and beyond in the early 1960s.

Tim Jenkins was a black student leader present at the NSA congress where I was encouraged to run for national vice president but thought it better for Tim to run instead. He later became a leader and helped with training for SNCC orientations. Tim's leadership was important in giving black students leverage in the national student movement during the early 1960s.

Ella Baker was a central figure in SNCC and my work with the organization. I first met her through YWCA activities and later worked directly for her, traveling to southern campuses to recruit students for race relations workshops. Ella was always present in Atlanta during the height of SNCC's southern efforts, and I took minutes at many meetings she attended. She was a mentor and leader who shaped much of my civil rights work in the 1960s.

Ann Arbor was a key place in my life where I visited Tom Hayden and helped organize a food drive and fundraiser for SNCC in western Tennessee. Later, Tom went back to school there, and I took a teaching assistantship at the University of Illinois, hoping to be closer to him, though it was still hours away. Ann Arbor was also a hub for SDS activity and student activism during the early 1960s.

I took a teaching assistantship at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign in the mid-1960s and ran the Y there. I thought it would bring me closer to Tom Hayden, who was in Ann Arbor, but it was still quite far. While at Illinois, I continued my activism and organizing, balancing my academic work with my civil rights commitments.

I was involved with SDS from its early days, meeting many of its founders at the NSA congress where the liberal caucus formed. Tom Hayden was the SDS secretary, and we both participated in SDS activities including conventions and organizing efforts. SDS was intertwined with the civil rights movement and student activism in the early 1960s, and I was present at the drafting of the Port Huron Statement around 1962.

My husband Tom and I spent a summer in New York City involved with SDS activities. We attended the National Student Association conference there and participated in study groups and organizing efforts. New York was one of the key urban centers where student activism and civil rights organizing intersected during the early 1960s.

Atlanta was a major hub for SNCC and southern civil rights organizing where I worked closely with Ella Baker. I traveled there often for meetings, took minutes, and helped organize race relations workshops on campuses. It was also where Bob Moses’s southern Mississippi efforts were gaining momentum, and where I lived and worked during parts of the early to mid-1960s.

I lived and worked in Mississippi as part of SNCC’s literacy project and the Mississippi Summer Project starting in the early 1960s. We stayed at the Freedom House in Jackson and worked on self-instruction literacy programs to empower local communities. Mississippi was a dangerous but vital place for civil rights activism, and I was there for the beginnings of the Freedom Love and other key efforts through 1966.

I participated in the Freedom Rides, including trips to Albany, where Tom and I sat in courtrooms supporting the movement. These rides were part of the broader civil rights efforts to challenge segregation in the South during the early 1960s. Being part of the Freedom Rides connected me directly with SNCC and other activists fighting for racial justice.

I was present at the drafting of the Port Huron Statement around 1962, which was a foundational document for SDS. It captured the ideals and goals of the student movement at that time, and I was deeply involved with both SDS and civil rights activism then. The statement helped define the direction of student activism for years to come.

I served as the northern coordinator for Friends of SNCC, organizing campus groups, sending out mailings, and helping build support in cities like New York and Chicago. This role allowed me to connect northern students with SNCC’s work in the South and helped raise awareness and resources for the movement. It was an important part of bridging regional activism during the mid-1960s.

Freedom House in Jackson, Mississippi, was where I lived and worked during the SNCC literacy project starting in the early 1960s. It was a base for organizing and training, and we stayed there until 1966. Freedom House was central to SNCC’s efforts to empower local communities through education and activism.

Toogoloo College was a site where summer staff from Radcliffe and Harvard, including Elaine Baker, came to support the Mississippi Summer Project in 1963 and 1964. Though I wasn’t directly part of Toogoloo, it was connected to the broader SNCC and civil rights work I was involved in. The college was important as a center for training and organizing during the summer projects.

Radcliffe College students, including Elaine Baker, came down to Mississippi to be summer staff at Toogoloo College during the civil rights summer projects in the early 1960s. Their involvement helped support SNCC’s organizing and literacy efforts. Radcliffe was one of the northern institutions whose students participated in southern civil rights work.

Students from Harvard University, alongside those from Radcliffe, joined the Mississippi Summer Project as summer staff at Toogoloo College in 1963 and 1964. Their participation was part of the broader northern student involvement in SNCC and civil rights organizing. Harvard was one of the institutions contributing to the movement through student activism in the South.

Mary King was a collaborator on a memo about women’s roles and second-class citizenship within the movement, which we worked on together at the literacy house. She later wrote a book about the civil rights movement, and I helped with some of the writing for her and for Tom. Mary was active in Mississippi and part of the network of women who shaped the movement’s history, even if communication was sometimes limited between regions.

Ruby Doris Smith-Robinson was involved in the civil rights movement and connected to the group that worked on the memo about women’s issues, though there was some confusion about authorship. She was a significant figure in SNCC and the women’s leadership within the movement. I knew her name and contributions as part of the broader struggle for equality and recognition of women activists.

Sarah is an author who reached out to me while writing a book about the civil rights movement. She wanted to talk to me about the memo and the history of women’s roles in SNCC and the movement. Her work prompted me to reflect on how much of the history has been forgotten or misremembered, and the importance of getting firsthand stories from those involved.

Elaine Baker was a key figure who came down from Radcliffe and Harvard to help with the Mississippi Summer Project and was present at important meetings in Jackson and Atlanta. She helped write the memo about women’s roles and was deeply involved in the literacy house and organizing efforts. Elaine’s contributions were significant, though she herself had not thought much about the history until recently, highlighting how much of the movement’s story has been lost or overlooked.

The Mississippi Summer Project was a major civil rights campaign in which I participated through SNCC starting in the early 1960s. It involved organizing voter registration, literacy programs, and community empowerment, with staff from northern colleges coming to help. The project was dangerous but vital, and it brought together activists from across the country to challenge segregation and disenfranchisement in Mississippi.
